summary: ggk-happy: rebranded fork silently redirects auth/RPC to undisclosed operator with remote shell handlers and unpinned binary dropper
details: ggk-happy is a rebranded fork of the upstream slopus/happy project. package.json, README, homepage (happy.engineering), repository, and author metadata all reference the upstream project, and README instructs users to `npm install -g happy` and run `happy claude`, but the shipped bin is `ggkhappy`/`ggkhappy-mcp` and the hardcoded defaults route to an undisclosed operator: DEFAULT_SERVER_URL is `https://happy-api.ask-ggk.com` and DEFAULT_WEBAPP_URL is `https://happy.ask-ggk.com`, while README declares the default as `https://api.cluster-fluster.com`. Installers who follow the documented QR-code auth flow are silently onboarded onto ask-ggk.com. On load the CLI opens a persistent socket.io session to that server and registers RPC handlers including `bash` (execAsync on arbitrary command strings), `readFile`, `writeFile`, `listDirectory`, `getDirectoryTree`, `ripgrep`, and `difftastic`, giving the server operator remote shell and filesystem access over any authenticated installer. The CLI additionally downloads platform-specific `rtk` binaries from `https://minio.ask-ggk.com/happy/rtk-*` (Windows/macOS/Linux variants) with no checksum or signature verification, extracts them to `~/.local/share/ggkhappy/rtk/bin/rtk` (or %LOCALAPPDATA%), executes them, and POSTs the output plus machineId, auth token, platform, and version fields to `https://guguke.ask-ggk.com/api/v1/agent/rtk/gain/report`. Machine metadata (hostname, platform, arch, homedir path) and the session auth token are also POSTed to `happy-api.ask-ggk.com`. A `postinstall` script (`scripts/postinstall.cjs`) auto-invokes `node bin/happy.mjs install` with inherited stdio when the package is installed globally or as root (opt-out via HAPPY_SKIP_AUTO_TAKEOVER=1), bootstrapping the persistent ask-ggk.com connection and RTK dropper without user interaction. None of ask-ggk.com, guguke.ask-ggk.com, or minio.ask-ggk.com are disclosed in README, package.json, or publisher metadata.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
